title: Meta Senior Manager (M2) on Manager Career Growth, PIPs, Amazon vs Meta | Stefan Mai
guest_role: Former Meta Senior Manager (M2), ex-Amazon SDM, co-founder of Hello Interview

sections.title: Growth to eng manager at Amazon
Senior manager (M2) promo story at Meta
Can you get out of a PIP?

key_takeaways: Mai's first week at Amazon (2012, early self-service advertising) began with his manager committing the team to a 6-8 month nights-and-weekends death march; 50-60 hour weeks were fairly common for ambitious big-tech engineers.

His manager transition came prematurely and took ~18 months of failing; the core early mistake was not knowing how to get reports to reveal their real goals, fixed via active listening instead of transactional API-style one-on-ones.

Storytelling is a bedrock manager skill: narrate the team's trajectory and define winning, because a team that feels it's winning avoids zero-sum politics, which fester exactly when winning slows down.

Manager-of-managers is a different job with real 'mutiny' risk: in flat orgs, leaf managers advance by displacing those above them via skip-level griping; and two teams given overlapping charters will undercut each other rather than run a clean experiment.

Senior leaders have fewer, more binary levers: you cannot do an underperforming manager's job for them, and at the top (e.g. Zuckerberg with an SVP) underperformance simply means being let go, not coached.
Managers are not layoff-proof: companies target manager-to-IC ratios and span-of-control reductions put managers first on the chopping block.

Amazon and Meta performance systems converge: public ratings plus a private VP/director process (Amazon 'top tier', Meta discretionary equity) picks linchpins by trajectory and irreplaceability; Meta pays its top quintile dramatically more (some cleared 2x peers) while frugal Amazon pulls that back.

The performance process costs roughly $15,000 per engineer per cycle by his own estimate, but 'the integrity of the performance review process is the whole ball game' - if people stop believing it rewards performance, they stop performing.

Low-performer quotas are universal at scale: roughly 7-15% (up to 20%) get pushed into the bottom bucket ('needs improvement' at Amazon, 'meets some/meets most' at Meta), where PIPs engage; the nightmare is a director forcing a manager to retroactively downgrade someone they praised.

PIPs are first a legal paper trail, but people genuinely escape them because the trail loses legal credibility if nobody survives; Amazon HR was savage box-checking while Meta HR exhausted every avenue to protect its employer reputation.

AI cheating has made remote interviews 'horrible' and focus/copy-paste detection only catches the unsophisticated; he expects assessments to evolve (low-level design, AI-allowed assignments) rather than anti-cheat to win, and notes cheaters get destroyed by ruthless performance management once hired.

For OpenAI/Anthropic interviews: OpenAI notoriously reuses question areas, and behavioral rounds reward raw technical achievement - the staff-engineer 'seven docs and six aligned teams' story lands poorly there.

Job hopping helps ambitious juniors but is a red flag at senior levels (staff+ moving every 18-24 months gets second-guessed); conversely, deep tenure's credibility snowball can become a shackle if you're forever known for one system.

Advice to younger self: find mentors instead of going alone, and write explicit plans with high agency - walking into his director's office with a list of things he didn't yet know how to do was the moment his director knew he'd succeed.

notable_quotes.speaker: Stefan Mai
notable_quotes.quote: I've got an idea. This is what we're gonna build, and we're gonna work nights and weekends until it happens.

notable_quotes.speaker: Stefan Mai
notable_quotes.quote: If you assume that leading other managers is going to be leading engineers, then you are going to fail very quickly. For a lot of people, it'll just be mutiny.

notable_quotes.speaker: Stefan Mai
notable_quotes.quote: The integrity of the performance review process is the whole ball game. If you get that wrong and people don't believe the process is gonna produce outcomes for them, they're not going to perform.

notable_quotes.speaker: Stefan Mai
notable_quotes.quote: I think PIPs are first and foremost a legal paper trail, but in order to maintain credibility of that legal paper trail, people have to make it through it.
